attrs.alt: Feature flags gate GenAI changes through rollout tiers (internal 1% → beta 10% → gradual 50% → full 100%) with eval gate at each tier

content.text: Why Trunk Stability Requires Feature Flags for GenAI

Trunk-based development mandates that main is always deployable. For traditional software, this means feature toggles that hide incomplete UI or business logic. For GenAI, it means something more demanding: the ability to merge a new system prompt, a new agent behaviour, or a new tool call into main and deploy it without it activating for all users. Without feature flags, every prompt change is a live change the moment it merges. That breaks the separation between 'code is deployed' and 'feature is active for users', which is essential for GenAI where production eval of a new prompt often reveals regressions that staged testing did not.

Feature flags in GenAI systems serve three overlapping purposes: they decouple deploy from activate, they enable percentage rollouts that let you compare old and new prompt behaviour on live traffic, and they provide a kill switch that can disable a misbehaving agent in seconds without a rollback deploy.
The Flag Client

The flag client wraps your feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or a static config for tests) behind a stable interface. All GenAI feature flag keys are defined as constants in one class so that renaming a key is a single-file change, and the defaults are the safe fallback — always the old behaviour. A flag that defaults to True is a flag that cannot be disabled in a degraded state, which is dangerous for an agent.
Testing with Feature Flags

Every function that branches on a feature flag must be tested in both the enabled and disabled state. Using the offline mode and the override mechanism makes flag-controlled paths fully testable without network calls or a running LaunchDarkly instance. The test suite below covers defaults, overrides, and flag isolation between clients.
The Rollout Plan Document

Every GenAI feature flag that reaches production must have a rollout plan. The rollout plan defines the rollout tiers, the eval gates between tiers, the rollback procedure for each tier, and the flag cleanup timeline. Without a rollout plan, flags accumulate — every old flag is a branch in the code that must be understood before the next change. The rollout plan also makes the kill switch protocol explicit so that it can be executed under pressure without discussion.

# Rollout Plan: genai.new-agent-v2
# Status: approved for rollout  Date: 2025-03-10

## Flag Details
- Key:       genai.new-agent-v2
- Type:      boolean
- Owner:     @ai-platform-team
- Jira:      AIP-214
- PR:        #312 (merged 2025-03-08)

## Rollout Tiers

### Tier 0 — Internal (1%)
- Target:    Internal users only (email domain: corp.example.com)
- Duration:  48 hours
- Eval gate: Pass rate ≥ 95%, no safety regression, latency p99 < 1500ms
- Rollback:  Flag off immediately, post incident in #ai-incidents

### Tier 1 — Beta (10%)
- Target:    Beta cohort users (attribute: beta_user = true)
- Duration:  72 hours
- Eval gate: Same as Tier 0, plus user satisfaction survey delta ≥ 0
- Rollback:  Flag off, notify beta users via in-app banner

### Tier 2 — Gradual (50%)
- Target:    Random 50% of production users, excluding enterprise accounts
- Duration:  7 days
- Eval gate: Same as Tier 1, plus no increase in support tickets about agent errors
- Rollback:  Flag to 0%, post to #ai-incidents, page on-call SRE

### Tier 3 — Full (100%)
- Target:    All production users
- Duration:  Permanent
- Post-rollout action: Remove old agent_v1 code path, schedule flag cleanup within 30 days

## Kill Switch
- Who can flip: AI Platform on-call or Engineering Lead
- Escalation: If on-call cannot be reached, any engineer in #ai-platform can flip
- Post-kill action: Incident retrospective within 24 hours

## Flag Cleanup
After Tier 3 is stable for 30 days:
- Remove flag check from route_agent_request
- Remove agent_v1 module
- Delete flag from LaunchDarkly
- Close this rollout plan document
Eval Gates Between Rollout Tiers

A rollout tier advance is not automatic. Before moving from Tier 0 (internal) to Tier 1 (beta), the team must evaluate live traffic metrics against the eval gate criteria: eval pass rate, safety regression check, latency, and user satisfaction. These gates are checked manually by the feature owner and recorded in the rollout plan document. Automated tier advancement (continuous delivery pipelines that auto-advance tiers when metrics pass) is an option for mature teams, but the criteria must still be explicit — automating a vague criterion produces a false sense of safety.
Eval pass rate: run the offline eval suite against the new prompt on the traffic slice that has the flag enabled. A pass rate below 95% blocks advancement.
Safety regression: run the red-team eval suite against the active prompt. Any new safety failure blocks advancement regardless of overall pass rate.
Latency: p99 latency on flag-enabled traffic must remain within the SLA. Prompt changes that add significant tokens frequently increase latency.
User satisfaction: for consumer-facing agents, compare user satisfaction scores between the flag-on and flag-off cohorts. A statistically significant negative delta blocks advancement.
Support ticket volume: monitor support tickets mentioning the agent for the 48 hours after each tier advance. A spike in agent-related tickets warrants investigation before proceeding.
Writing Code That Respects the Flag Contract
A flag check must be exactly one thing: a boolean decision at one point in the call path. The anti-patterns below are the most common sources of trunk instability in GenAI systems.

Flag checked in multiple places: if five functions each check the same flag independently, they can disagree when the flag value changes mid-request. Evaluate the flag once at the request boundary and pass the resolved value as a parameter.

Flag logic in the prompt file: embedding if/else logic inside the prompt template using template variables is not a feature flag — it is an untested code branch inside a string. Use separate prompt files for each variant, controlled by a single flag at the router level.

Flags that default to the new behaviour: a flag whose default is True cannot be safely disabled without explicit action. Defaults must be the old (safe) behaviour so that a flag service outage degrades to the previous state.

Flags that are never cleaned up: a codebase with 40 active feature flags is a codebase with 40 untested code branches. Flag cleanup is part of the feature delivery contract, not optional housekeeping.

Flag checks inside eval code: eval code should test the actual prompt file, not a flag-controlled variant. Flag-controlled code paths are tested by activating the flag in a test FlagClient, not by reading the flag inside the eval runner.
Kill Switch Protocol

Every GenAI feature flag must be usable as a kill switch. This means: the on-call engineer must be able to disable the flag from a phone or a laptop in under two minutes, with no code deployment required. The kill switch is the most important safety property of the flag system. Test it explicitly during every internal tier rollout: flip the flag off, verify the old behaviour is active, flip the flag back on, verify the new behaviour returns. If the kill switch does not work cleanly, do not advance to the next tier.
Identify the flag key for the misbehaving feature from the rollout plan document.
Log in to the flag service console (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or your internal dashboard).
Set the flag to 0% (or off) for all targeting rules.
Verify that error rates, latency, and safety metrics return to baseline within 60 seconds.
Post to #ai-incidents: timestamp, flag key, what triggered the kill, and who performed the action.
Open a post-incident review within 24 hours to determine root cause and update the eval gates for the next rollout attempt.
Flag Audit: Preventing Stale Flags

A codebase with many feature flags gradually accumulates stale flags — flag key strings that exist in code but have been removed from the flag service, or registered in GenAIFlags but no longer used in any code path. Stale flags produce two failure modes: a code path that always takes the else branch because the flag is gone (silently defaulting), and registered constants that mislead future developers into thinking the feature is still behind a flag when it has already been permanently enabled.

The following audit script scans the codebase for GenAI flag key strings, compares them to the registered constants in GenAIFlags, and reports both unregistered usages and unused registered keys. Run this in CI as a pull request check to prevent new stale flags from accumulating.
Integrating Flag Checks Into the Development Workflow
Feature flags only stabilize the trunk if developers actually use them consistently. The following checklist makes flag discipline a pull request requirement rather than a personal habit.
Every PR that adds or changes GenAI behaviour must add the corresponding flag key to GenAIFlags and set a safe default.
Every PR that removes a GenAI code path because a flag rollout is complete must also remove the flag key from GenAIFlags and delete the flag from the flag service.
The flag audit script must pass in CI for every PR that touches any file containing a genai. flag key string.
Rollout plan documents must be stored in docs/rollout-plans/ and linked from the PR that introduces the flag.
Flag cleanup work must be scheduled as a Jira ticket at the time the Tier 3 (full) rollout completes, with a due date 30 days out.
